Overview.content: Every agent call ‚Äî whether orchestrator, discovery, planning, ticket, data-layer operation, or controller conversation ‚Äî flows through the same execution pipeline. The pipeline is structured as a **four-stage decorator chain** (request ‚Üí prompt ‚Üí tool ‚Üí result) orchestrated by `AgentExecutor`, with `BlackboardHistory` providing per-session state persistence and `DecorateRequestResults` coordinating the decorator collections. Understanding this pipeline is essential because every observable behavior (events, graph node creation, worktree attachment, prompt assembly, error recovery) is implemented as a decorator, not inline in agent code.

The Execution Pipeline.content:
When an Embabel `@Agent` route (e.g., `DiscoveryRoute`, `OrchestratorRoute`) needs to call the LLM, it calls `AgentExecutor.run()`. This method executes a strict 9-step process:

```
Agent Route (@Agent @Action)
 ‚îÇ
 ‚ñº
AgentExecutor.run(AgentExecutorArgs)
 ‚îÇ
 ‚îú‚îÄ 1. REQUEST DECORATION ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ DecorateRequestResults.decorateRequest()
 ‚îÇ      sorted by order():          or .decorateResultsRequest() for ResultsRequest types
 ‚îÇ      EmitActionStartedRequestDecorator (MIN_VALUE ‚Äî runs first, emits ActionStartedEvent)
 ‚îÇ      RegisterBlackboardHistoryInputRequestDecorator (records input to history)
 ‚îÇ      SetGoalRequestDecorator (writes goal onto blackboard)
 ‚îÇ      WorktreeContextRequestDecorator (attaches worktree paths)
 ‚îÇ      PropagateActionRequestDecorator (runs pre-action data-layer propagators)
 ‚îÇ      StartWorkflowRequestDecorator (10000 ‚Äî creates/updates graph nodes via WorkflowGraphService)
 ‚îÇ
 ‚îú‚îÄ 2. TEMPLATE MODEL PRODUCTION ‚îÄ TemplateModelProducer lambda from the route
 ‚îÇ
 ‚îú‚îÄ 3. PROMPT CONTEXT BUILD ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ PromptContextFactory.build() + PromptContextDecorators
 ‚îÇ      DefaultPromptContextDecorator, FilterPropertiesDecorator
 ‚îÇ      ‚Üí produces PromptContext (agent type, template, chat ID, error state)
 ‚îÇ
 ‚îú‚îÄ 4. TOOL CONTEXT BUILD ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ ToolContextDecorators
 ‚îÇ      AddAcpTools, AddTopologyTools, AddSkillToolContextDecorator,
 ‚îÇ      AddMemoryToolCallDecorator, AddIntellij / RemoveIntellij
 ‚îÇ
 ‚îú‚îÄ 5. EXECUTOR START EVENT ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ publishes AgentExecutorStartEvent (unique nodeId per attempt)
 ‚îÇ
 ‚îú‚îÄ 6. ERROR TEMPLATE RESOLUTION ‚îÄ resolveTemplate() checks BlackboardHistory.errorType()
 ‚îÇ      if last error was CompactionError ‚Üí use compaction template
 ‚îÇ      if ParseError ‚Üí use parse error template, etc.
 ‚îÇ      ErrorTemplates are per-action metadata on AgentActionMetadata
 ‚îÇ
 ‚îú‚îÄ 7. LLM CALL ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ LlmRunner.runWithTemplate()
 ‚îÇ      LlmCallDecorators run first (PromptHealthCheckLlmCallDecorator, ArtifactEmissionLlmCallDecorator)
 ‚îÇ      ‚Üí assembles prompt via PromptContributorService
 ‚îÇ      ‚Üí calls ACP chat model
 ‚îÇ
 ‚îú‚îÄ 8. EXECUTOR COMPLETE EVENT ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ publishes AgentExecutorCompleteEvent (same nodeId links to start)
 ‚îÇ
 ‚îî‚îÄ 9. RESULT DECORATION ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ DecorateRequestResults.decorateRouting()
        WorkflowGraphResultDecorator (updates graph nodes: pending/complete via WorkflowGraphService)
        PropagateActionResultDecorator (runs post-action propagators)
        EmitActionCompletedResultDecorator (MAX_VALUE ‚Äî runs last, emits ActionCompletedEvent)
```

The `order()` on each decorator controls sequencing. `EmitActionStarted` runs at `MIN_VALUE` (first); `StartWorkflow` at `10000` (after enrichment); `EmitActionCompleted` at `MAX_VALUE` (last, with the fully decorated result).

Request/Result Decorator Symmetry.content:
The request and result sides are mirror images that jointly manage the graph node lifecycle:

**Request side** (`StartWorkflowRequestDecorator`): Pattern-matches on the request type (via exhaustive `switch`) and calls the corresponding `WorkflowGraphService.start*()` method. For example, `DiscoveryOrchestratorRequest` ‚Üí `startDiscoveryOrchestrator()` ‚Üí creates/reuses the node ‚Üí marks it RUNNING. For `InterruptRequest` subtypes, it calls `handleAgentInterrupt()` instead.

**Result side** (`WorkflowGraphResultDecorator`): Pattern-matches on the routing type and calls the corresponding `WorkflowGraphService.pending*()` or `complete*()` method. Routing types that carry an `interruptRequest` field get interrupt handling. Agent results (Discovery/Planning/TicketAgentRouting) resolve the running node by looking up the original request's `contextId` from the `DecoratorContext`.

This pairing means every agent call goes through: **request decorator creates/starts node ‚Üí LLM runs ‚Üí result decorator completes/pends node**. The graph is always consistent because both sides are in the same pipeline.

The Fan-Out Dispatch Process.content:
For dispatched agents (discovery/planning/ticket), `AgentExecutor` has dedicated methods (`runDiscoveryDispatch`, `runPlanningDispatch`, `runTicketDispatch`) that implement the fan-out/fan-in pattern:

1. Decorate the container request (e.g., `DiscoveryAgentRequests`)
2. Iterate over individual requests, running each as a **subprocess** via `ActionContext.asSubProcess()` ‚Äî this triggers the Embabel framework to run the dispatch sub-agent (`WORKFLOW_DISCOVERY_DISPATCH_SUBAGENT`, etc.)
3. If any subprocess returns an interrupt, abort and propagate the interrupt routing
4. Collect all results into a `ResultsRequest` (e.g., `DiscoveryAgentResults`)
5. Run the full `AgentExecutor.run()` pipeline with the collected results as input ‚Äî this is the **consolidation LLM call** that produces the dispatch routing

The key insight: each subprocess is itself a full Embabel agent execution that goes through its own decorator pipeline (including graph node creation for the leaf `DiscoveryNode`/`PlanningNode`/`TicketNode`). The consolidation step is a separate LLM call that sees all results pretty-printed via `AgentPretty`.

BlackboardHistory: Session State and Loo.content:
`BlackboardHistory` is the per-session memory that ties the decorator pipeline together. It lives on the Embabel `Blackboard` (accessed via `OperationContext`) and serves three critical functions:

1. **Action history**: Every request and result passes through `RegisterBlackboardHistoryInputRequestDecorator`, which calls `history.addEntry()`. This creates a chronological log of `Entry` records (action name, input object, timestamp, input type). The history is how decorators like `StartWorkflowRequestDecorator` can find the "last workflow request" ‚Äî it walks the entries backwards, skipping interrupt/communication/utility requests.

2. **Workflow graph state**: `WorkflowGraphState` is stored on the history and tracks the current node IDs for each phase role (orchestrator, dispatch, collector for discovery/planning/tickets). `WorkflowGraphService` reads and writes this state via `resolveState()` / `updateState()`, which access the blackboard through the `OperationContext`.

3. **Error tracking and loop detection**: Errors are recorded as `ErrorDescriptor` entries. The history tracks `AgentExecutorStartEvent` / `AgentExecutorCompleteEvent` pairs to identify retry sequences ‚Äî if a start has no matching complete, the agent is being retried. `errorCountForRetrySequence()` counts errors within the current retry chain (not across completed executions). Loop detection uses `detectLoop()` with a configurable threshold (default 3) to prevent degenerate agent loops.

Controller Conversation Pipeline.content:
When an agent calls the controller (via `AgentTopologyTools.callController`), a parallel pipeline runs:

1. `AgentExecutor.controllerExecution()`: Runs the full decoration chain (request ‚Üí prompt ‚Üí prompt contributors) but **instead of calling the LLM**, publishes an interrupt and blocks via `InterruptService.publishAndAwaitControllerInterrupt()`. The fully rendered prompt becomes the interrupt content.

2. `AgentExecutor.controllerResponseExecution()`: When the controller responds, this runs the decoration chain for `ControllerToAgentRequest`, renders the response through prompt contributors, then resolves the original interrupt via `PermissionGate.resolveInterrupt()`.

The key hierarchy: `interruptId` = `callingKey.createChild()` (set when the agent initiated the call). The controller response creates a child contextId under the interrupt, and the `chatId` points to the agent's session (so the response goes to the right LLM conversation).

Error Recovery and Retry.content:
When the LLM fails (parse error, timeout, compaction, null result), the Embabel framework retries via `ActionRetryListener`. On retry:

1. `ErrorDescriptor` is recorded on the blackboard history (by the retry infrastructure)
2. Next `AgentExecutor.run()` call detects the error via `history.errorType(sessionKey)` ‚Äî scoped to the current retry sequence
3. `resolveTemplate()` replaces the normal Jinja template with an error-specific template from `ErrorTemplates` (e.g., `compactionFirstTemplate`, `parseErrorTemplate`)
4. The error template model contains `errorDetail` from the exception message
5. Compaction errors have two tiers: `compactionFirstTemplate` (first occurrence) and `compactionMultipleTemplate` (repeated compaction failures)

The retry sequence tracking is sophisticated: `findFirstUnmatchedStartIndex(sessionKey)` walks the history to find the chain of `AgentExecutorStartEvent` entries without matching `AgentExecutorCompleteEvent` entries (matched by unique `nodeId`, not `requestContextId` which is shared across retries).

**ACP-layer retry context** ‚Äî `AcpRetryEventListener` maintains a parallel per-session retry state (`AcpSessionRetryContext`) at the ACP transport layer. It subscribes to lifecycle events (`ChatSessionCreatedEvent`, `AgentExecutorStartEvent/CompleteEvent`, `CompactionEvent`, `NullResultEvent`, `IncompleteJsonEvent`, `UnparsedToolCallEvent`, `TimeoutEvent`) and tracks `ErrorCategory` + `CompactionStatus` per session. `AcpChatModel` queries this context to decide whether the current call is a retry and which error category triggered it ‚Äî this allows the ACP layer to adjust its behavior (e.g., session management, timeout tuning) independently of the Embabel-level retry template selection. The two layers are complementary: Embabel retry controls *what prompt* the agent sees; ACP retry context controls *how the transport* behaves.

AgentInterfaces and Metadata Registry.content: `AgentInterfaces.MultiAgentIdeMetadata.AgentMetadata` is a static registry of all agent actions. Each `AgentActionMetadata<T, U, V>` record captures: agent name, action name, method name, agent type, template path, error templates, and the request/routing/result type parameters. This is how the system knows which template to use, which error templates apply, and how to type-check the decorator pipeline for any given action. The `findByAction()` method looks up metadata at runtime (used by `runDirect()` for callers that already have their own contexts).

Communication Topology.content:
The topology system controls which agents can communicate during execution. CommunicationTopologyConfig (YAML-driven) defines allowed communication pairs per AgentType, maxCallChainDepth, and messageBudget. CommunicationTopologyProvider wraps the config for runtime queries. AgentCommunicationService enforces the topology: listAvailableAgents filters by topology-permitted, session-open, and not-self; validateCall rejects disallowed pairs. The reachableVia field enables indirect routing ‚Äî if agent A cannot call B directly, it reports which intermediaries can reach B. AgentTopologyTools (list_agents, call_agent, call_controller) are the MCP tools agents use to communicate, each annotated with @SetFromHeader(MCP_SESSION_HEADER) for session identity.

**Agent checklists** ‚Äî The controller skill maintains per-agent-type checklists in `conversational-topology/` that define the review contract for each phase gate. `checklist.md` is the general phase-gate review checklist (applies to all agents). Agent-specific checklists: `checklist-orchestrator.md`, `checklist-discovery-orchestrator.md`, `checklist-discovery-dispatch.md`, `checklist-discovery-agent.md`, `checklist-discovery-collector.md`, `checklist-planning-orchestrator.md`, `checklist-planning-dispatch.md`, `checklist-planning-agent.md`, `checklist-planning-collector.md`, `checklist-ticket-orchestrator.md`, `checklist-ticket-dispatch.md`, `checklist-ticket-agent.md`, `checklist-ticket-collector.md`, `checklist-orchestrator-collector.md`. Always load the general checklist AND the agent-specific checklist before approving any call_controller conversation.

Blackboard Planner.content:
`BlackboardRoutingPlanner` replaces the default Embabel GOAP planner with a fast type-matching router. Instead of evaluating preconditions across all actions, it reads the last blackboard entry's type and matches it against each action's input bindings:

1. `BlackboardRoutingPlannerFactory.create()` registers the planner with `DefaultAgentPlatform`
2. On each routing step, `planToGoal()` reads the current `BlackboardWorldState`
3. If the last entry is an `AgentModels.Routing` (a `SomeOf` record), it extracts the first non-null record component as the actual routed request
4. Matches the extracted type against each action's `inputBindings` via `satisfiesType()`
5. Returns a single-action `ConditionPlan` ‚Äî no multi-step planning

This is why every `@Action` method in the workflow agents has a `when` condition that checks a specific routing field ‚Äî the planner dispatches based on type matching, not condition evaluation.
